response.description_long:
Gridmaple targets the largest energy load in most commercial buildings — HVAC — with software alone. Its diagnosis: buildings run on static schedules programmed years ago, so they heat empty floors, pre-cool on mild days, and fight themselves between zones, while the BMS faithfully executes instructions from 2016. The pitch is optimization without retrofit, riding the building management system already installed.

The mechanism is a closed loop. Gridmaple connects over BACnet to the major BMS platforms — Johnson Controls Metasys, Siemens Desigo, Honeywell — with no sensors to install; onboarding is a points-mapping exercise measured in weeks. Machine-learning models trained on the building's own history forecast occupancy and weather per zone and compute an optimized schedule every 15 minutes: later starts on slow days, shallower pre-cooling when the afternoon will be mild, staggered equipment starts that avoid demand spikes. Changes write back within guardrails the facilities team sets — temperature bands, cycling limits, excluded systems — and every adjustment is logged and reversible, with a one-click return to the original schedule.

Money is optimized alongside kilowatt-hours. Gridmaple loads the building's tariff — time-of-use windows, demand charge structure — and shifts load away from peak pricing, shapes demand-response participation, and packages meter-verified evidence for utility rebate programs, which the site says fund part of some customers' subscriptions.

The results claim is 15 to 30 percent energy savings on HVAC, and the verification method is the credibility argument: measured against utility meters, weather-normalized, with the baseline drawn from the building's own metered history. The same data feeds emissions disclosures per building per month.

Customers are owners and operators of office, retail, and healthcare portfolios, from a single 80,000-square-foot building to portfolios of 200 sites; retail estates run a remote-first rollout templated from prototype store designs, and clinical spaces in healthcare are excluded by guardrail. Deployment runs from BACnet connection through observe mode to closed-loop optimization in about five weeks, with full savings ramping over the first quarter. Pricing is per building per year, sized by floor area — a flat subscription rather than a shared-savings contract.

Gridmaple was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York City, at 52 people. The founding trio maps to the product's two competencies: controls engineers Peter Voss and Miguel Santos, who spent years watching commissioning-day optimizations decay, and Hannah Cole, a former utility analyst who priced commercial tariffs and knew what static schedules cost. The wedge remains the no-hardware claim — portfolio-scale deployment at software speed, while competing paths require sensor retrofits and capital budgets.
